title: Introduction to Modal Logics - Kripke Semantics

charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of modal logic syntax, Kripke semantics, validity hierarchies, the modal logic K, and the labelling algorithm for model checking.

nodes.label:
Week 9
Modal Logic
nodes.note: Week 9 introduces modal logic, the modal operators box and diamond, Kripke semantics, the minimal modal logic K, and how to model-check modal formulas.

nodes.label: Motivation
nodes.note: Natural reasoning often involves words like possibly and necessarily that are not truth-functional. Classical propositional logic cannot express such modes of truth.

nodes.label:
Non-truth-
functional
nodes.note: There are only four truth tables for a unary connective and none of them captures possibility or necessity. This forces a richer semantic framework.

nodes.label:
Modal Formula
Syntax
nodes.note: Modal formulas extend propositional formulas by allowing box F and diamond F as new well-formed expressions for any modal formula F.

nodes.label:
Kripke
Models
nodes.note: A Kripke model M = (W, R, V) consists of a set of worlds W, an accessibility relation R between worlds, and a propositional valuation V at each world.

nodes.label: Frames
nodes.note: A Kripke frame is just (W, R), without the valuation. Validity over a frame quantifies over all valuations.

nodes.label: Satisfiability
nodes.note: M, w models F means F is true at world w in model M. Diamond F holds at w iff F holds at some R-successor; Box F holds iff F holds at all R-successors.

nodes.label:
Validity
Hierarchy
nodes.note: Validity comes in increasing strength: at a world, over a model, over a frame, over a class of frames. Each step universally quantifies over more components.

nodes.label: Logic K
nodes.note: The minimal modal logic K consists of all modal formulas valid in every Kripke frame. It is sound and complete with respect to its axiomatic proof system.

nodes.label:
Frame
Properties
nodes.note: Reflexive frames validate Box p to p (axiom T); transitive frames validate Diamond Diamond p to Diamond p (axiom 4). Different frame classes characterise different modal logics.

nodes.label:
Labelling
Algorithm
nodes.note: Bottom-up procedure for computing W(F), the set of worlds where F is satisfied. Subformulas are processed in order of increasing size.

nodes.label: W(F) Notation
nodes.note: W(F) = { w in W : V(w, F) = 1 } is the set of worlds satisfying F. It enables divide-and-conquer model checking.

nodes.label:
Completeness
of K
nodes.note: Theorem 9.6: semantic consequence and provability in the proof system for K coincide. The axiom Box(p to q) to (Box p to Box q) is a key K-tautology.

charts.title: Evaluating M, w models F
charts.description: Recursive procedure for computing the truth value of a modal formula at a single world of a Kripke model.

nodes.label:
Given M = (W,R,V),
world w, formula F
nodes.note: We are given a Kripke model, a specific world, and a modal formula. The goal is to decide whether the formula is satisfied at that world.

nodes.label:
Top connective
of F?
nodes.note: Determine the outermost connective of F. Each case applies a different sub-procedure to compute the truth value.

nodes.label: Look up V(w,p)
nodes.note: If F is a propositional variable p, simply look up the value of V at the pair (w, p) given by the model.

nodes.label:
Recurse on G,
then flip
nodes.note: If F = not G, recursively evaluate G at w and return its complement: 1 - V(w, G).

nodes.label:
Recurse on both,
then combine
nodes.note: If F is a binary connective and, or, or implication, recursively evaluate both subformulas at w and combine using the appropriate Boolean function.

nodes.label:
Check successors
for F holding
nodes.note: If F = diamond G, look at every R-successor u of w and check if G holds at any of them. If yes, F is true at w; otherwise it is false.

nodes.label:
Check all successors
for F holding
nodes.note: If F = box G, check that G holds at every R-successor of w. Vacuously true if w has no successors.

nodes.label:
Return truth
value at w
nodes.note: Return the computed truth value of F at world w. By recursion this gives a complete decision procedure for satisfiability at a single world.

charts.title: Labelling Algorithm for W(F)
charts.description: Bottom-up procedure for computing the set of all worlds where a modal formula is satisfied in a given Kripke model.

nodes.label:
Model M and
formula H
nodes.note: We start with a Kripke model and a target modal formula. We will compute W(H), the set of worlds where H is satisfied.

nodes.label:
Pre-process: rewrite
imp and box
nodes.note: Eliminate implication via not p or q and box via not diamond not, leaving only not, and, or, and diamond as connectives.

nodes.label:
List subformulas
in size order
nodes.note: Sort subformulas of H by size, smallest first. Each subformula will be processed exactly once and its W set stored.

nodes.label:
More subformulas
to process?
nodes.note: Iterate through the sorted list of subformulas. Once all are processed we have W(H) at the top of the list.

nodes.label: Subformula type?
nodes.note: Identify the connective of the next subformula in order to apply the corresponding case of the labelling algorithm.

nodes.label: W(p) = {w : V(w,p)=1}
nodes.note: For a propositional atom p, the set W(p) is read directly from the valuation V.

nodes.label: W(not F) = W \ W(F)
nodes.note: For negation, take the set complement: any world not in W(F) belongs to W(not F).

nodes.label:
W(F and G) =
W(F) cap W(G)
nodes.note: For conjunction, intersect the W sets of both subformulas. A world satisfies F and G iff it satisfies both.

nodes.label:
W(F or G) =
W(F) cup W(G)
nodes.note: For disjunction, take the union. A world satisfies F or G iff it satisfies at least one of them.

nodes.label:
W(diamond F) =
{w : exists v in W(F), wRv}
nodes.note: For diamond, collect all worlds that have at least one R-successor in W(F). This is the predecessor closure of W(F) under R.

nodes.label: Output W(H)
nodes.note: When all subformulas have been processed, the set W(H) gives the worlds where H is satisfied. Validity over M holds iff W(H) = W.

charts.title: Validity Hierarchy in Modal Logic
charts.description: Classification of the four levels of validity in modal logic and how each level quantifies over additional components.

nodes.label:
Validity
Levels
nodes.note: Modal logic has four nested notions of validity, each quantifying over progressively more parameters of the model.

nodes.label:
At a world
M, w models F
nodes.note: The most local notion: F is satisfied at the specific world w of model M. Nothing is universally quantified.

nodes.label:
Over a model
M models F
nodes.note: F is satisfied at every world of M. We universally quantify over the worlds of the fixed model.

nodes.label:
Over a frame
F models F
nodes.note: F is satisfied in every model built on the frame F. We universally quantify over both worlds and valuations.

nodes.label:
Over a frame class
C models F
nodes.note: F is valid in every frame from a chosen class. This is how axioms like T or 4 characterise specific modal logics.

nodes.label:
Logic K =
all frames
nodes.note: The minimal modal logic K is the set of formulas valid over the class of all frames. Axiom K = box(p to q) to (box p to box q) is a member.

nodes.label:
Logic KT
= reflexive frames
nodes.note: Adding reflexivity gives logic KT, validating axiom T = box p to p. Modal logic of necessity that is true.

nodes.label:
Logic K4
= transitive frames
nodes.note: Adding transitivity gives logic K4, validating axiom 4 = diamond diamond p to diamond p.

charts.title: Verifying a Frame-Property Correspondence
charts.description: Procedure to test whether a candidate axiom corresponds to a given frame property such as reflexivity or transitivity.

nodes.label:
Candidate axiom A
and frame property P
nodes.note: We want to show that A is valid in exactly those frames satisfying property P, e.g. T = Box p to p with reflexivity.

nodes.label:
Find a frame WITHOUT P
where A fails
nodes.note: Construct a small counter-model that does not satisfy P and shows A failing somewhere. This proves A is not valid in all frames.

nodes.label:
Counter-example
found?
nodes.note: Check whether a valid counter-model has been produced. If yes, A is not valid over all frames; otherwise we may need to reconsider.

nodes.label:
Cannot establish
correspondence
nodes.note: Without a counter-example outside P, A might already be a K-theorem and the property P is unnecessary.

nodes.label:
Suppose P holds
on a frame
nodes.note: Move to the soundness direction: assume P holds on the frame and any model on it. We will show A is valid there.

nodes.label:
Assume A fails
at some w in M
nodes.note: For contradiction, suppose there is a world where the candidate axiom A is not satisfied, despite the frame having property P.

nodes.label:
Use property P
to derive contradiction
nodes.note: Combine the assumption that A fails with the structural property P (reflexivity, transitivity, etc.) to derive a contradiction.

nodes.label:
A is valid on every
frame with P
nodes.note: The contradiction shows that A holds at every world of every model on a frame satisfying P, completing the correspondence proof.
